examples.title: Linear Congruential Generator Implementation
examples.description: Basic LCG algorithm showing the recurrence relation for pseudorandom number generation
examples.explanation: This is a simple LCG implementation. The parameters shown are from the 'MINSTD' generator. While computationally efficient O(1), it is NOT cryptographically secure because the linear structure allows parameter recovery via Berlekamp-Massey algorithm.

examples.title: Blum Blum Shub Generator
examples.description: Cryptographically secure PRNG based on quadratic residues modulo composite n
examples.code:
FUNCTION BBS_SETUP(p, q):
 // p, q are primes ≡ 3 (mod 4)
 n ← p * q
 seed ← random_prime(n)
 state ← (seed * seed) MOD n
 RETURN (n, state)
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION BBS_GENERATE(n, state, bits):
 outputs ← empty_list
 FOR i = 1 TO bits DO
 state ← (state * state) MOD n
 // Extract least significant bit
 output_bit ← state AND 1
 APPEND output_bit TO outputs
 END FOR
 RETURN outputs
END FUNCTION

// Example usage:
p ← 7 // In practice: 512-bit prime
q ← 11 // In practice: 512-bit prime
(n, state) ← BBS_SETUP(p, q)
random_bits ← BBS_GENERATE(n, state, 128)
examples.explanation: BBS security relies on the integer factorization problem. Computing square roots modulo n = p·q is computationally infeasible without knowing p and q. Only 1-2 LSBs are extracted per iteration to maintain security properties.

examples.title: CTR_DRBG (Counter Mode PRNG)
examples.description: NIST standardized cryptographic PRNG using block cipher in counter mode
examples.code:
FUNCTION CTR_DRBG_INstantiate(entropy, personalization):
 key ← 0x00...00 // 128 or 256 bits
 counter ← 0
 seed_material ← entropy ⊕ personalization
 key ← KDF(key, seed_material) // Key derivation
 RETURN (key, counter, 0) // (key, counter, reseed_counter)
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION CTR_DRBG_GENERATE(key, counter, requested_bits):
 output ← empty_string
 blocks ← ceil(requested_bits / block_size)
 
 FOR i = 1 TO blocks DO
 block ← AES_ENCRYPT(key, counter)
 output ← output || block
 counter ← counter + 1
 END FOR
 
 // Update internal state
 key ← AES_ENCRYPT(key, counter)
 counter ← counter + 1
 
 RETURN (output[0:requested_bits], key, counter)
END FUNCTION

// Generate 256 random bits:
entropy ← get_system_entropy() // 256+ bits
(key, counter, _) ← CTR_DRBG_INstantiate(entropy, "app-specific-string")
(random_bytes, key, counter) ← CTR_DRBG_GENERATE(key, counter, 256)
examples.explanation: CTR_DRBG is a NIST standard offering efficient, parallelizable random bit generation. Forward security is achieved as each output updates the key. Backward security requires periodic reseeding from entropy source. Parallelizable due to counter increment pattern.
